title: tsdb/wlog: fix shadowed metadata slice decoding in Checkpoint
author: vishalmore90
body:
### What did you do?

During TSDB WAL checkpointing, the `Checkpoint` function in `tsdb/wlog/checkpoint.go` reads records from the WAL segments. For `Metadata` records:
1. The `metadata` slice is decoded at line 361.
2. The outer `metadata` variable is shadowed due to the use of `:=` inside the `case record.Metadata:` block:
   ```go
   case record.Metadata:
       metadata, err := dec.Metadata(rec, metadata)
   ```

All other case blocks use normal assignment `=` (e.g. `series, err = dec.Series(rec, series)`) to update the preallocated outer variable and reuse its capacity.



### What did you expect to see?

The outer variable `metadata` should be reused across loop iterations using assignment `=` rather than short variable declaration `:=`, avoiding a new slice allocation per metadata record.
Also, the `stats.DroppedMetadata` statistic should accurately track the number of dropped metadata records.



### What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

A fresh slice allocation is made for every metadata record processed during checkpointing. In addition, the `stats.DroppedMetadata` count is computed incorrectly because the inner shadowed variable's length is subtracted from `repl` (which increments on unique references, not total records).

This happens whenever Prometheus runs a WAL checkpoint (compactions or manual TSDB cleanups) when metadata records are present in the WAL.


### System information

OS: Linux / Windows / Any

### Prometheus version

```text
# Standard configuration
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
```

### Prometheus configuration file

```yaml
# Standard configuration
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
```

### Alertmanager version

```text
N/A
```

### Alertmanager configuration file

```yaml
N/A
```

### Logs

```text
N/A
```

title: Label values endpoint POST method
author: eduardscaueru
body:
### Proposal

Currently, the [label values endpoint](https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/querying/api/#querying-label-values) only supports the **GET** method, but it allows matchers. For long matchers (hardcoded values of some labels that cannot be in the same request or other use cases) we might get URI too long.

Can we add the **POST** method to this endpoint just like [labels](https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/querying/api/#getting-label-names) and [series](https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/querying/api/#finding-series-by-label-matchers) endpoints to avoid URI too long?

title: Add test case for histogram_quantile annotation
author: roidelapluie
body:
LGTM, nit: test would be nice to have

engine_test.go
```
func TestHistogramQuantilesForcedMonotonicityAnnotationPosition(t *testing.T) {
	// A non-monotonic classic histogram triggers the forced monotonicity info
	// annotation. Its position range must point at the histogram vector
	// (histogram_quantiles's first argument), not at the label string literal.
	store := promqltest.LoadedStorage(t, `load 1m
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="0.1"}   0+2x10
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="1"}     0+1x10
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="10"}    0+5x10
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="100"}   0+4x10
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="1000"}  0+9x10
		nonmonotonic_bucket{le="+Inf"}  0+8x10
	`)
	t.Cleanup(func() { _ = store.Close() })

	engine := promqltest.NewTestEngineWithOpts(t, promql.EngineOpts{
		MaxSamples:               promqltest.DefaultMaxSamplesPerQuery,
		Timeout:                  100 * time.Minute,
		NoStepSubqueryIntervalFn: func(int64) int64 { return int64(1 * time.Minute / time.Millisecond / time.Nanosecond) },
		EnableDelayedNameRemoval: true,
		Parser:                   parser.NewParser(promqltest.TestParserOpts),
	})

	// The vector argument starts at column 21, right after "histogram_quantiles(".
	expr := `histogram_quantiles(nonmonotonic_bucket, "q", 0.5)`
	query, err := engine.NewInstantQuery(context.Background(), store, nil, expr, timestamp.Time(0).Add(1*time.Minute))
	require.NoError(t, err)
	t.Cleanup(query.Close)

	res := query.Exec(context.Background())
	require.NoError(t, res.Err)

	_, infos := res.Warnings.AsStrings(expr, 0, 0)
	require.Len(t, infos, 1)
	require.Contains(t, infos[0], "input to histogram_quantile needed to be fixed for monotonicity")
	require.True(t, strings.HasSuffix(infos[0], "(1:21)"), "annotation should point at the histogram vector, got: %s", infos[0])
}
```

_Originally posted by @krajorama in https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/pull/18852#pullrequestreview-4671389767_

title: textparse: ProtobufParser.Next() recurses on empty/skipped metric families, risking stack overflow (DoS)
author: vishalmore90
body:
### Context

The Protocol Buffer parser (`ProtobufParser`) in `model/textparse/protobufparse.go`
processes metric families using recursive calls to `p.Next()` in multiple places.
When encountering empty metric families, ignoring native histograms, or switching
between native and classic histogram representations, the parser recurses rather
than looping. This allows the call stack to grow linearly with the number of
skipped entries.
PR #19143 ("fix(parsing): Parse metrics without recursion") recently fixed the
identical issue in `openmetricsparse.go` and `promparse.go` by converting those
`return p.Next()` calls into `continue` inside a top-level `for` loop. The
`ProtobufParser` was **not** included in that fix and remains vulnerable.
A malicious or misconfigured scrape target serving `application/vnd.google.protobuf`
can trigger a stack overflow crash in the Prometheus server by returning a payload
containing a large number of empty metric families.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Build a binary that runs the following (or stand up a target that returns the
   corresponding protobuf payload):

```go
package main

import (
    "bytes"
    "encoding/binary"
    "fmt"
    "runtime/debug"

    "github.com/gogo/protobuf/proto"
    "github.com/prometheus/prometheus/model/textparse"
    dto "github.com/prometheus/prometheus/prompb/io/prometheus/client"
)

func main() {
    // Restrict stack to 1 MB to surface the overflow quickly.
    debug.SetMaxStack(1024 * 1024)

    mf := &dto.MetricFamily{
        Name: "empty_metric",
        Help: "no samples",
        Type: dto.MetricType_GAUGE,
        // No Metric entries -- intentionally empty.
    }
    protoBuf, _ := proto.Marshal(mf)

    const numEmpty = 20_000
    varintBuf := make([]byte, binary.MaxVarintLen32)
    buf := &bytes.Buffer{}
    for i := 0; i < numEmpty; i++ {
        n := binary.PutUvarint(varintBuf, uint64(len(protoBuf)))
        buf.Write(varintBuf[:n])
        buf.Write(protoBuf)
    }

    parser := textparse.NewProtobufParser(
        buf.Bytes(), false, false, false, false, nil,
    )
    fmt.Printf("Calling Next() on %d empty metric families\n", numEmpty)
    entry, err := parser.Next()
    fmt.Printf("entry=%v err=%v\n", entry, err)
}
```

2. Run the binary.

### Expected Result

The parser iterates over empty/skipped metric families without growing the call
stack and returns `(EntryInvalid, io.EOF)` when the payload is exhausted.


### Repo Configuration

Minimal Prometheus scrape configuration targeting an endpoint that returns
`Content-Type: application/vnd.google.protobuf` with many empty metric families:

```yaml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'test'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['malicious-or-misconfigured-target:8080']
    scrape_protocols:
      - PrometheusProto
```

---

### Log Output

```
Created parser with 20000 empty metric families. Starting Next()...
Alloc before Next: 12566 KB
runtime: goroutine stack exceeds 1048576-byte limit
fatal error: stack overflow
```

### Impact

- **Availability (DoS):** Any scraped target (external service, compromised
  exporter, or a misconfigured one emitting only type/help metadata with no
  data) can crash the entire Prometheus server process.
- **Severity:** Medium-High. No authentication is required; any target reachable
  by the scraper can exploit this.

```release-notes
[BUGFIX] textparse: Fix stack overflow in ProtobufParser.Next() when parsing
payloads with many empty or skipped metric families.
```

title: ui: add native histogram support in Mantine UI
author: vishalmore90
body:
## Description
The Prometheus UI (Mantine UI) lacks native histogram support in the PromQL query editor and related components. Several TODO comments in the codebase indicate that native histogram handling is not yet implemented (e.g., `// TODO: Implement native histogram support.` in `web/ui/mantine-ui/src/promql/binOp.ts`). Attempting to write or execute queries that involve native histograms (e.g., using `histogram_quantile` with native histogram data) results in UI errors or missing functionality. This gap affects users trying to explore native histogram data via the web UI.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run Prometheus from source (`make run` or `./prometheus`) and access the UI at `http://localhost:9090`.
2. Open the **Graph** page and enter a PromQL query that references a native histogram metric, such as:
   ```promql
   histogram_quantile(0.9, rate(my_native_histogram_bucket[5m]))
   ```
3. Click **Execute** or press **Enter** to run the query.
4. Observe that the UI either displays an error (e.g., “Unsupported operation” or “Unexpected token”) or fails to render the result, and the console logs contain warnings related to missing native histogram handling.



### What did you expect to see?

## Expected Result
The UI should correctly parse, validate, and display results for queries involving native histograms, providing a seamless experience similar to scalar and counter queries.

### What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

## Additional Context
- **Root cause analysis**: The UI code contains explicit TODO placeholders for native histogram support in multiple files:
  - `web/ui/mantine-ui/src/promql/binOp.ts:443` – “TODO: Implement native histogram support.”
  - `web/ui/mantine-ui/src/promql/utils.ts` – various TODOs around histogram handling.
- **Impact**: Users cannot visualize or query native histogram data via the UI, limiting observability for newer metric types.



### System information

Prometheus

### Prometheus version

```text
Go 1.22
```

### Prometheus configuration file

```yaml
# Clone and build Prometheus
git clone https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus.git
cd prometheus
make build   # or `make run` to start the server
# Access UI at http://localhost:9090
```

### Logs

```text
## Log Output
(Excerpt from browser console when reproducing the issue)

[Error] Native histogram support not implemented in PromQL editor.
    at binOp.ts:443
    // TODO: Implement native histogram support.
```

title: `promtool: --enable-feature=promql-binop-fill-modifiers` is not recognized, causing `fill()` rule validation to always fail
author: vishalmore90
body:
### Description

`promtool` does not recognise the `promql-binop-fill-modifiers` feature flag in its
`--enable-feature` switch statement.  As a result, users who write recording rules or
alerts with `fill()` / `fill_left()` / `fill_right()` binary-operator modifiers and then
try to validate those rules with `promtool check rules --enable-feature=promql-binop-fill-modifiers`
receive a misleading "WARNING: Unknown feature …" message and the parse fails with
_"binop fill modifiers are experimental and not enabled"_.

---

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Create a recording rule file `fill_rule.yml`:

```yaml
groups:
  - name: example
    rules:
      - record: job:requests_per_status
        expr: |
          rate(successful_requests[5m]) + fill(0) rate(failed_requests[5m])
```

2. Run promtool with the feature flag:

```
promtool check rules --enable-feature=promql-binop-fill-modifiers fill_rule.yml
```

**Expected:** validation passes (same as when Prometheus itself starts with the flag).

**Actual output:**
```
  WARNING: Unknown feature passed to --enable-feature: promql-binop-fill-modifiers
  FAILED:
    fill_rule.yml: 1 error(s):
      ...expr: [1:34] binop fill modifiers are experimental and not enabled
```

### What did you expect to see?

## Suggested Fix

Add `"promql-binop-fill-modifiers"` to the feature switch in
[`cmd/promtool/main.go`](file:///C:/Users/Vishal/Code_File/Repo%20Folder/prometheus/cmd/promtool/main.go#L353-L369):

```diff
        case "promql-extended-range-selectors":
            promtoolParserOpts.EnableExtendedRangeSelectors = true
+       case "promql-binop-fill-modifiers":
+           promtoolParserOpts.EnableBinopFillModifiers = true
        case "":
            continue
```

A matching test should be added to
`cmd/promtool/unittest_test.go` (or a dedicated test) that:
1. Creates a rules file with a `fill()` expression.
2. Calls `CheckRules` / `RulesUnitTest` with `--enable-feature=promql-binop-fill-modifiers`.
3. Asserts no error is returned.

---

## Affected Files

| File | Line(s) | Change |
|------|---------|--------|
| [`cmd/promtool/main.go`](file:///C:/Users/Vishal/Code_File/Repo%20Folder/prometheus/cmd/promtool/main.go#L353-L369) | 353–369 | Add `case "promql-binop-fill-modifiers"` |
| `cmd/promtool/*_test.go` | — | Add regression test |


### Expected behaviour

`promtool check rules --enable-feature=promql-binop-fill-modifiers` should parse
and validate `fill()` expressions the same way that the Prometheus server does when
started with the same flag.



### What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

## Impact

* Users cannot validate fill-modifier rules/alerts with `promtool` before deploying.
* CI pipelines that run `promtool check rules` as a pre-flight check will always
  reject queries that are valid on the live server.
* The flag is otherwise completely ignored — no warning is emitted in other promtool
  subcommands that also construct a parser (e.g. `promtool query instant`).

### System information

go 1.25.0

### Prometheus version

```text
Prometheus 3.12
```

### Prometheus configuration file

```yaml
groups:
  - name: example
    rules:
      - record: job:requests_available
        expr: |
          rate(successful_requests[5m]) + fill(0) rate(failed_requests[5m])
```

### Alertmanager version

```text

```

### Alertmanager configuration file

```yaml

```

### Logs

```text

```

title: Deltas: Confirm support for ST-per-sample in OTLP endpoint
author: ywwg

title: tsdb: Close() errors silently discarded in Querier and blockChunkQuerierForRange cleanup
author: om7057
body:
I noticed a couple of cleanup paths in `tsdb/db.go` where errors returned from `Close()` are currently ignored.

This happens in `Querier()` and `blockChunkQuerierForRange()`:

```go
for _, q := range blockQueriers {
    _ = q.Close()
}
```

These cleanup defers run while handling an error path from the surrounding function. If one of the queriers also fails during `Close()`, that information is currently discarded, so the caller only sees the original error.

One possible improvement would be to preserve both errors using `errors.Join()` so the cleanup failure is not lost, for example:

```go
if closeErr := q.Close(); closeErr != nil {
    err = errors.Join(err, fmt.Errorf("close querier during cleanup: %w", closeErr))
}
```

This would keep the original failure while still surfacing any cleanup issues that happen while closing the queriers.

title: docs/configuration: oauth2.http_headers listed as supported
author: emilykmarx
body:
The configuration documentation for [`oauth2`](https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/configuration/configuration/#oauth2) lists a section for `http_headers`, but as far as I can tell `http_headers` isn't supported as a section of `oauth2`.

I tested by editing the testdata for one of the config load tests:
Adding an `http_headers` section to the `oauth2` section under  `remote_write` in [conf.good.yml](https://github.com/prometheus/prometheus/blob/40af9c2cdc0eda00f3622e867a27f6359f7295f3/config/testdata/conf.good.yml#L40) causes `TestLoadConfig` to fail with:
```
        Error:      	Received unexpected error:
        	            	parsing YAML file testdata/conf.good.yml: yaml: unmarshal errors:
        	            	  line 40: field http_headers not found in type config.plain
```


The `remote_write` section of the edited conf.good.yml is:
```
  - url: http://remote1/push
    name: drop_expensive
    write_relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: [__name__]
        regex: expensive.*
        action: drop
    oauth2:
      client_id: "123"
      client_secret: "456"
      token_url: "http://remote1/auth"
      tls_config:
        cert_file: valid_cert_file
        key_file: valid_key_file
      http_headers:
        foo:
          values: ["foobar"]
          secrets: ["bar", "foo"]
          files: ["valid_password_file"]

  - url: http://remote2/push
    protobuf_message: io.prometheus.write.v2.Request
    name: rw_tls
    tls_config:
      cert_file: valid_cert_file
      key_file: valid_key_file
    headers:
      name: value
```

title: Remove the old, React UI
author: roidelapluie
body: The React UI is deprecated. Let's remove it with the major version bump.

title: enable `__name__` label removal delay for PromQL engine
author: roidelapluie
body: Let's make this the default for Prometheus 4.0 and remove the feature flag.

title: Make XOR2 the default Chunk Encoding
author: roidelapluie
body: Let's switch Float Chunk Encoding to XOR2 by default and mark it as stable. Remove the feature flag. Keep XOR, configurable with the tsdb: config.yml (configuration is already done).

title: Use distroless image by default
author: roidelapluie
body:
Let's tag :distroless as the new `latest` and `main` images.

Keep Busybox image for now.

title: Prometheus 4.0 Roadmap
author: roidelapluie
body:
Some proposals for Prometheus 4.0

- Use distroless image by default (keep busybox)
- Remove old ui
- enable `__name__` label removal delay for PromQL engine
- Make XOR2 encoding the default. (ST still disabled)

title: govulncheck: GO-2026-5662 (Prometheus Stored XSS) reported in metrics agent
author: mjnowen
body:
### What did you do?

# govulncheck: GO-2026-5662 (Prometheus Stored XSS) reported in metrics agent

## Summary

A CI `govulncheck` run reports my project's Go metrics agent as affected by
**[GO-2026-5662](https://pkg.go.dev/vuln/GO-2026-5662)** via a transitive
dependency on `github.com/prometheus/prometheus`.

- **Vulnerability:** GO-2026-5662
- **Aliases:** CVE-2026-40179, GHSA-vffh-x6r8-xx99
- **Affected module:** `github.com/prometheus/prometheus@v0.313.0`
- **Fixed in:** N/A (no fixed version currently mapped)
- **Scanner:** [govulncheck v1.5.0](https://pkg.go.dev/golang.org/x/vuln/cmd/govulncheck)
- **Detected by:** CI job (`go1.26.4` standard library)

## Description

> Prometheus has Stored XSS via metric names and label values in Prometheus
> web UI tooltips and metrics explorer in `github.com/prometheus/prometheus`.

More info: <https://pkg.go.dev/vuln/GO-2026-5662>

## Assessment

The finding is a transitive dependency pulled in via the OpenTelemetry
Collector Prometheus exporter / remote-write exporter components. The reported
call traces are dominated by package `init` functions and generated
component/test code, not by any use of the vulnerable Prometheus **web UI**
(tooltips / metrics explorer), which is where the Stored XSS actually lives.

This project does not embed or serve the Prometheus web UI, so the vulnerable
code path is very likely **not reachable** in practice. This looks like a
probable **false positive** for this project's usage.

> **Note:** If this is confirmed to be a false positive, the upstream Go
> vulnerability report should be edited accordingly via the "Suggest an edit"
> link on <https://pkg.go.dev/vuln/GO-2026-5662>. The report is currently marked
> **Unreviewed** and was automatically generated from a third-party source.

## Reproduction

```console
$ govulncheck -test -show verbose ./...

### What did you expect to see?

_No response_

### What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

```
=== Symbol Results ===
Vulnerability #1: GO-2026-5662
    Prometheus has Stored XSS via metric names and label values in Prometheus
    web UI tooltips and metrics explorer in github.com/prometheus/prometheus
  More info: https://pkg.go.dev/vuln/GO-2026-5662
  Module: github.com/prometheus/prometheus
    Found in: github.com/prometheus/prometheus@v0.313.0
    Fixed in: N/A
    Example traces found:
      #1..#129: [REDACTED — project init/component/test call stacks
                 eventually reaching prometheus annotations/labels/prompb/
                 remote-write symbols]
=== Package Results ===
No other vulnerabilities found.
=== Module Results ===
No other vulnerabilities found.
Your code is affected by 1 vulnerability from 1 module.
This scan found no other vulnerabilities in packages you import or modules you
require.
```

### System information

Ubuntu 24.04, Ubuntu 26.04, Linux kernel 7.1.2

### Prometheus version

```text
v3.13.0
v0.313.0 (golang package)
```

### Prometheus configuration file

```yaml

```

### Alertmanager version

```text

```

### Alertmanager configuration file

```yaml

```

### Logs

```text

```

title: tsdb: silent self-deadlock in mmapHeadChunks during panic or I/O failure
author: Siddhartha-singh01
body:
### What did you do?

We investigated a recurring issue where the Prometheus TSDB could experience a silent, unrecoverable self-deadlock during chunk memory mapping (`mmapHeadChunks`), specifically when a panic or catastrophic I/O failure occurs (e.g., when `handleChunkWriteError` triggers a panic in tests, or a disk goes fully offline).

We have already implemented and verified a fix locally by extracting the nested loops into `mmapHeadChunksInStripe` and `mmapSeriesChunks` to leverage `defer` for safe lock release.


### What did you expect to see?

If `mmapChunks()` encounters a write error and panics, the process should crash or recover cleanly, releasing all acquired mutexes (`h.series.locks[i]` and `series.Lock()`) so that deferred cleanup tasks (like `t.Cleanup()` calling `Head.Close()`) can execute without deadlocking the entire test runner or TSDB closer.


### What did you see instead? Under which circumstances?

A permanent self-deadlock. 

In `tsdb/head.go` within the `mmapHeadChunks` loop, two locks are acquired sequentially:
1. `h.series.locks[i].RLock()`
2. `series.Lock()`

Currently, these locks are released using explicit `RUnlock()` and `Unlock()` calls at the bottom of the loop body rather than being protected by `defer`. If `series.mmapChunks` panics, the loop exits immediately, skipping the unlock instructions. This leaks both the stripe lock and the series lock. 

Subsequently, when cleanup routines (or any concurrent operations) attempt to safely close the TSDB Head by calling `mmapHeadChunks()` again, they block forever waiting for those identical locks, resulting in CI timeouts (particularly visible on 386 architectures) or process hangs.


### System information

Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu) x86_64

### Prometheus version

```text
prometheus, version HEAD (branch: main)
```

### Prometheus configuration file

```yaml
# N/A - Core TSDB concurrency bug, not configuration dependent.
```

### Alertmanager version

```text
N/A
```

### Alertmanager configuration file

```yaml
# N/A
```

### Logs

```text
(No logs from production; manifests as silent deadlocks and test timeouts during local/CI test runs for `TestDiskFillingUpAfterDisablingOOO`).
```
